reasoning_trace.control_strategy: Keep all non-laser power settings fixed across all conditions.; Use the same NiTi powder lot, argon shielding setup, and sample geometry for every run.; Prepare every sample with the same sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing workflow before SEM imaging.

final_proposal.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.
final_proposal.hypothesis: Within the previously validated operating window, changing laser power will change porosity.
controls: Keep all non-laser power settings fixed across all conditions.
Use the same NiTi powder lot, argon shielding setup, and sample geometry for every run.

conditions: Laser power = 90% of the validated midpoint setpoint
Laser power = 100% of the validated midpoint setpoint
Laser power = 110% of the validated midpoint setpoint
procedure_outline: Run one DED build at each explicit numeric laser power condition within the previously validated stable operating window.
Keep the same powder lot, shielding setup, geometry, and all non-target process settings across conditions.
Section each build with the precision saw, then mount, grind, and polish for cross-section imaging.
Acquire SEM cross-sections for every replicate and record the porosity measurement for each sample.
Use XRD only as a secondary sanity check if phase consistency needs confirmation.

measurement_plan: Quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections for every replicate using one consistent image-analysis workflow.
Record the exact numeric laser power level and replicate identifier for every sample.
Summarize porosity by condition before comparing the low, midpoint, and high settings.
analysis_plan: Compute the mean and spread of porosity for each numeric laser power level.
Plot porosity versus laser power to check for a monotonic or non-linear trend.
Use the first-pass result to decide whether a narrower follow-up sweep is justified.

Keeps all non-target settings fixed and includes replicated SEM-based measurements.
